title: Variance: What PCA is Actually Looking For
concept: PCA hunts for the direction with the highest variance (spread), because a direction where data points differ a lot from each other carries the most information about them.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does PCA build to summarize a dataset?

options.text: New directions (combinations of the original features) that capture the most variation
A ranked list of the original columns, best to worst
A set of clusters grouping similar rows together

exercises.explain: Right! PCA builds new combined directions. But how does it decide which directions are worth keeping?

cards.title: Why one photo angle beats another
cards.body: Photograph a pencil end-on, and the picture shows almost just a dot — you can't tell how long it is. Photograph it from the side, and the picture shows its full length clearly. The side angle captures far more information about the pencil's true size.

cards.title: PCA's search: maximum variance
cards.body: PCA doesn't guess randomly. It tests different directions through the data and measures the variance (spread) of the points along each one. The direction with the highest variance becomes the first principal component, because more spread means the points differ from each other more along that direction — exactly the kind of information a model needs to tell data points apart.

cards.title: A security camera aimed at a statue
cards.body: Point a camera straight down at a statue and its shadow looks like a small, similar-sized blob no matter which statue you're looking at — low variance, hard to tell them apart. Point the camera from the side and the shadow shows the statue's full silhouette — high variance, easy to tell statues apart. PCA always prefers the angle that spreads things out the most.

cards.title: Sizing up a basketball team
cards.body: A dataset tracks each player's shoe size and height. Shoe sizes are almost all the same across the team — low variance, not useful for telling players apart. Heights range from 5'8" to 7'0" — high variance, very useful for distinguishing players. PCA would favor a direction built around height over one built around shoe size.

cards.title: More variance isn't just "bigger numbers"
cards.body: It's tempting to think PCA picks whichever feature has the biggest raw numbers, like a salary in dollars beating an age in years. In reality, PCA cares about relative spread, not raw scale — a feature only looks high-variance because of its units otherwise. That's why data is usually scaled first (as in the Feature Scaling unit) before running PCA.

cards.title: High-variance direction vs. low-variance direction
cards.body: Comparing two possible directions through the same data:

left.text:
Points spread widely apart
Lots of distinguishing information

right.text:
Points bunched tightly together
Almost no distinguishing information

exercises.prompt: Why does PCA search for directions of maximum variance?

options.text: Because more spread means more information for telling data points apart
Because bigger numbers are always more important
Because it wants to find outliers to delete

exercises.explain: Right! High variance means the points differ from each other a lot along that direction — which is useful, distinguishing information.

exercises.prompt: True or false: PCA should typically be run on raw, unscaled data, since bigger-scale features are naturally more important.
exercises.explain: False! Features should usually be scaled first — otherwise a feature just looks high-variance because of its units, not because it's actually more informative.

exercises.sentence: PCA searches for the direction where the data's ___ is highest.
exercises.explain: PCA ranks directions by variance — how spread out the data points are along them.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best matches how PCA chooses its first direction?

options.text: A researcher scales 5 lab measurements, then finds the direction along which the measurements spread out the most, to use as PCA's first component
A researcher picks the lab measurement with the most decimal places
A researcher groups the lab samples into clusters based on similarity

exercises.explain: Correct! PCA scales the data, then finds the direction of maximum spread — that becomes its first principal component.

exercises.prompt: A dataset has "Country Code" (nearly identical for 95% of rows) and "Annual Purchases" (ranging widely from 2 to 500). Before running PCA, why should this data be scaled first?

options.text: Without scaling, the feature with a larger numeric range could dominate the variance just because of its units, not because it's actually more informative
Because PCA can only process one feature at a time
Because scaling automatically deletes all low-variance features
Scaling has nothing to do with PCA

exercises.explain: Exactly! Unscaled features with larger numeric ranges can artificially dominate variance, so scaling first ensures PCA judges informativeness fairly, not just raw magnitude.
